entries.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Booked orders, financed deployments and multi-GW campus commitments accumulate faster than NVIDIA can ship, sustaining a demand overhang.
entries.sensitivity: Hyperscaler and AI-cloud capex plans and the pace of Vera Rubin supply ramp.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM leadTimes normalize to historical norms for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
entries.mechanism: Advanced-packaging (CoWoS) and HBM capacity gate how much wafer and demand can be converted into shippable accelerators, so realizable revenue tracks packaging/HBM output.
entries.sensitivity: Packaging (Amkor/TSMC) and HBM capacity additions relative to the Vera Rubin ramp.
entries.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
entries.mechanism: AMD ramping Instinct/Helios to mass production creates a viable second source that hyperscalers can adopt to reduce NVIDIA dependence.
entries.sensitivity: Named hyperscaler adoption commitments and Helios production yield versus NVIDIA availability.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named ASIC program is cancelled or delayed by 12+ months.
entries.mechanism: Hyperscalers and large AI labs build custom accelerators for better performance-per-watt and NVIDIA independence, diverting incremental workloads off merchant GPUs.
entries.sensitivity: Custom-silicon perf-per-watt gains versus NVIDIA and whether labs run ASIC and merchant GPUs in parallel rather than substituting.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
entries.mechanism: Persistent supply-demand tightness lets GPU-cloud providers hold on-demand rental rates even as new capacity comes online.
entries.sensitivity: Pace of new rental capacity coming online versus incremental rental demand.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
entries.mechanism: Export licensing restricts NVIDIA sales into controlled regions, shrinking addressable merchant-GPU demand when policy tightens.
entries.sensitivity: U.S. licensing policy changes and whether controlled-region demand can be redirected to permitted geographies.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA discloses financing receivables rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a financed AI-cloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed order to cash revenue within a quarter.
entries.mechanism: Vendor financing lowers customers' capital barriers and pulls demand forward, but ties NVIDIA revenue recognition to its own credit extension and the solvency of financed AI-cloud buyers.
entries.sensitivity: Credit conditions and the underlying cash solvency of financed AI-cloud customers.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity
